title: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.3` outputs, weekly dashboard PDF, and tamper-evident archive of result snapshots in `index=audit_evidence` referenced by the control matrix row.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(e) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced ‚Äî Splunk UC-22.2.3: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure and Patch Management Tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.3' running on index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln" (cve, severity, plugin_name, host, first_found, last_fixed, state), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted ‚Äî SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Seed Tenable-shaped events where `state=Active`, severity is Critical, and `first_found` is older than eight days; the alert fires listing the host with `max_age` above the seven-day SLA and includes open_vulns counts.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest matching events for the same host but include an open exception record in `vuln_exception_register.csv` covering the plugin_id until next quarter; the scheduled search returns no rows after the lookup filter is applied.

dataSources: `index=vulnerability` (or `index=vuln`) `sourcetype=tenable:vuln` fields host, severity, first_found, state from Tenable Add-On for Splunk ([Splunkbase 4060](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4060)); complementary patch posture from `index=patch` `sourcetype=wsus:update` or `sourcetype=sccm:compliance` via Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SCCM ([Splunkbase 1599](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1599)) with BulletinID, ComplianceState ‚Äî map to CIM Vulnerabilities for board-level rollups.
app: Tenable Add-On for Splunk ([Splunkbase 4060](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4060)); Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SCCM (Splunkbase 1599) when correlating patch deployment; optional CIM Vulneraries data model acceleration on the search head.
spl:
index=vulnerability sourcetype="tenable:vuln" state="Active"
| eval host=coalesce(host, hostname, dns_name)
| eval first_found=coalesce(first_found, first_seen)
| eval age_days=round((now()-first_found)/86400, 1)
| eval sla_days=case(severity="Critical",7, severity="High",30, 1=1,90)
| eval sla_breach=if(age_days>sla_days, 1, 0)
| stats count as open_vulns, max(age_days) as max_age by host, severity
| where sla_breach=1
| sort - max_age
| table host, severity, open_vulns, max_age, sla_days
description: Surfaces active Tenable-plugin findings where the time since first_seen exceeds policy-based remediation deadlines per severity bucket for each host.
value: Compliance teams can hand auditors a repeatable report that shows which servers breached internal fix deadlines and for how long, instead of exporting ad-hoc CSV snapshots from the scanner console every quarter.
implementation: (1) Install Tenable Add-On (4060) and confirm events land in `index=vulnerability` with `state` populated. (2) Open **Settings ‚Üí Data Summary ‚Üí Sourcetypes** and verify whether your feed uses `first_seen` vs `first_found`. (3) Copy SLA constants into a macro `nis2_sla_severity(1)` so operations can change thresholds once. (4) For patch proof, mirror SCCM (1599) compliance into `index=patch` and schedule a follow-on correlation search. (5) Route alerts to SecOps on-call with the host CMDB owner field.
visualization: Table of hosts past SLA, bar chart of open_vulns by severity, line chart of rolling count of breached hosts over 90 days.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
‚Ä¢ **Tenable plugin false positives on legacy middle-ware** ‚Äî Distinguish: plugin ID flagged for years with formal VA waiver in ServiceNow. Suppress: lookup `vuln_exception_register.csv` with plugin_id, host, expiry_date.
‚Ä¢ **Test or scan labs outside production scope** ‚Äî Distinguish: host matches `lab_prefix` from CMDB. Suppress: `where NOT match(host,"^(lab|tst)-")` or join asset class lookup.
‚Ä¢ **Deferred patching inside approved cyber-maintenance blackout** ‚Äî Distinguish: CAB-approved freeze with CHG record. Suppress: time-bound exception table `patch_blackout_exceptions.csv` keyed on host and epoch window.
‚Ä¢ **Field mapping drift (`first_seen` null)** ‚Äî Distinguish: scanner upgrade renamed fields; age stays zero. Suppress: fix `props.conf` on indexers, interim filter `where isnotnull(first_found)`.
‚Ä¢ **Container hosts counted twice** ‚Äî Distinguish: duplicate assets for docker host vs cluster member. Suppress: dedup on `host` after `rex` normalization to lowercase FQDN.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964, alternative vulnerability scanner)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, change_request for patch tracking)
Splunk Add-on for Tenable (Splunkbase 4060, vulnerability detection data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Assets & Identities for asset enrichment)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) ‚Äî security in network and information systems acquisition, development, and maintenance, including vulnerability handling and disclosure. It provides continuous evidence that: (1) known vulnerabilities are tracked with SLA-based remediation timelines, (2) patch deployment is verified against the vulnerability scanner's detection data, and (3) overdue vulnerabilities are escalated before they become audit findings. NIS2 requires entities to have policies and procedures for vulnerability handling ‚Äî this UC operationalises those policies.
‚Ä¢ **Delineation from other vulnerability UCs**: UC-22.2.21 monitors risk analysis for essential entities (Tenable-focused, GRC-enriched). UC-22.2.22 monitors risk analysis for important entities (Qualys-focused, SLA-based). UC-22.2.27 tracks vulnerability disclosure SLA (time-to-triage for external reports). UC-22.2.28 monitors cyber hygiene baselines (EDR/MFA compliance via Tenable plugins). This UC (22.2.3) is the central patch management tracker ‚Äî it focuses on the lifecycle of known vulnerabilities from detection to remediation, with SLA enforcement.
‚Ä¢ **Splunk Add-on for Tenable** (Splunkbase 4060): configured to pull vulnerability data via the Tenable.io or Tenable.sc API. The modular input pulls `vuln` exports containing: `host.hostname` (the scanned asset), `plugin.id` (Tenable plugin ID), `plugin.name` (vulnerability description), `severity` (critical, high, medium, low, info), `state` (open, reopened, fixed), `first_found` (when the vulnerability was first detected), `last_found` (most recent detection), `cve` (associated CVE identifiers). Credentials: use an API key pair (access_key, secret_key) with "Can View" permissions.
‚Ä¢ **Patch SLA lookup**: `nis2_patch_sla.csv` with columns: `severity` (critical, high, medium, low), `sla_days` (remediation deadline in days from first detection ‚Äî e.g., critical=14, high=30, medium=90, low=180), `escalation_days` (days after which an overdue vulnerability is escalated ‚Äî e.g., critical=7 after SLA, high=14 after SLA). These SLAs should align with the organisation's vulnerability management policy, which NIS2 requires entities to maintain.
‚Ä¢ **Asset enrichment**: Splunk Enterprise Security's Assets & Identities framework enriches each vulnerability with asset metadata: `category` (server, workstation, network), `priority` (critical, high, medium, low), `bunit` (business unit), `owner` (asset owner email). This allows vulnerability prioritisation: a critical vulnerability on a critical server is more urgent than one on a low-priority workstation.
‚Ä¢ **ServiceNow integration**: the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) pulls `change_request` records for patch deployments. The UC correlates vulnerability detections with patch change tickets to verify: (a) a patch was deployed for the vulnerability, and (b) the vulnerability scanner confirms the patch was effective (state changed to "fixed").
‚Ä¢ **Exception handling**: `nis2_vuln_exception_register.csv` with columns: `plugin_id` (Tenable plugin), `host` (specific host or "*" for all), `exception_until` (ISO 8601 date), `reason` (e.g., "vendor dependency ‚Äî awaiting upstream patch", "compensating control in place ‚Äî WAF rule deployed", "risk accepted by CISO"), `approved_by` (who approved the exception). Vulnerability exceptions must be time-bound and approved ‚Äî indefinite exceptions are a compliance risk.
‚Ä¢ **RBAC**: role `nis2_vuln_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = vulnerability, itsm, audit_evidence`. Vulnerability data reveals exploitable weaknesses ‚Äî restrict access to the security team and patch management personnel.
‚Ä¢ **Index retention**: `index=vulnerability` ‚â• 1 year (trend analysis and audit evidence). `index=audit_evidence` ‚â• 7 years.

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
**Tenable vulnerability data:**
Install the Splunk Add-on for Tenable (Splunkbase 4060) on a Heavy Forwarder. Configure the modular input:
- API URL: `https://cloud.tenable.com` (Tenable.io) or `https://sc.internal.example.com` (Tenable.sc)
- Authentication: API access_key and secret_key stored in Splunk's credential store
- Data type: `vuln` (vulnerability detections)
- Interval: 3600 seconds (hourly)
- Index: `vulnerability`
- Sourcetype: `tenable:vuln`
- Filter: `state=open,reopened` (only active vulnerabilities ‚Äî fixed vulnerabilities are excluded to reduce volume)

Verify:
```
index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(host) as scanned_hosts dc(plugin_id) as unique_vulns values(severity) as severities
```
Expected: `scanned_hosts` matches the Tenable dashboard's "Assets" count. `severities` should include critical, high, medium.

**Patch SLA lookup:**
Place `nis2_patch_sla.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-vuln/lookups/`. Example content:
```csv
severity,sla_days,escalation_days
critical,14,7
high,30,14
medium,90,30
low,180,60
```
Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_patch_sla]
filename = nis2_patch_sla.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```

**ES asset enrichment:**
Verify asset data is flowing into ES: `| inputlookup asset_lookup_by_str | stats count dc(key) as assets`. Each vulnerability host should be enrichable via `| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS host OUTPUT category priority bunit owner`.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.3 NIS2 Vulnerability and Patch Management`.

Understanding this SPL ‚Äî design decisions:

‚Ä¢ **`state="Active"` or `state IN ("open","reopened")`**: filters to only vulnerabilities that are currently detected on the asset. Fixed vulnerabilities are excluded ‚Äî they are evidence of successful remediation and should be tracked separately for trend analysis.

‚Ä¢ **`eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(first_found,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"))/86400,0)`**: calculates how many days the vulnerability has been open since first detection. This is the core metric for SLA compliance ‚Äî if `age_days > sla_days`, the vulnerability has breached its remediation SLA.

‚Ä¢ **`lookup nis2_patch_sla.csv severity OUTPUT sla_days escalation_days`**: enriches each vulnerability with the applicable SLA based on its severity. A critical vulnerability with `sla_days=14` and `age_days=21` has been overdue for 7 days.

‚Ä¢ **`eval sla_breach=if(age_days > sla_days, 1, 0)`**: binary flag for SLA breach. `eval days_overdue=if(sla_breach=1, age_days - sla_days, 0)` calculates how many days past the SLA deadline.

‚Ä¢ **Asset enrichment**: `lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS host OUTPUT category priority bunit owner` adds business context. A critical vulnerability on a `priority=critical` server in `bunit=Finance` is prioritised over the same vulnerability on a `priority=low` workstation in `bunit=Marketing`.

‚Ä¢ **Exception register**: `lookup nis2_vuln_exception_register.csv plugin_id host OUTPUT exception_until reason approved_by | where isnull(exception_until) OR now() > strptime(exception_until, "%Y-%m-%d")` filters out accepted exceptions that haven't expired.

Schedule: cron `0 7 * * *` (daily at 07:00). Vulnerability SLAs are day-based ‚Äî daily cadence is appropriate. Time range: embedded `earliest=-30d`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For critical vulnerabilities with `days_overdue > escalation_days`: PagerDuty page to the CISO. (2) For high vulnerabilities with SLA breach: email to the patch management team lead. (3) Summary email to the vulnerability management team with counts by severity and SLA status. (4) Write all SLA breaches to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:vuln:sla_breach`.

Extended search ‚Äî patch management scorecard:
```spl
index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln state IN ("open","reopened","Active") earliest=-30d
| eval host=lower(coalesce(host, hostname, fqdn))
| lookup nis2_patch_sla.csv severity OUTPUT sla_days
| eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(first_found,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"))/86400,0)
| eval sla_status=case(age_days > sla_days, "OVERDUE", age_days > sla_days*0.8, "AT_RISK", 1==1, "ON_TRACK")
| stats dc(plugin_id) as total_vulns dc(eval(if(sla_status="OVERDUE",plugin_id,null()))) as overdue dc(eval(if(sla_status="AT_RISK",plugin_id,null()))) as at_risk dc(host) as affected_hosts by severity
| eval compliance_pct=round(100*(total_vulns-overdue)/total_vulns, 1)
| table severity total_vulns overdue at_risk affected_hosts compliance_pct
| sort case(severity="critical",1,severity="high",2,severity="medium",3,severity="low",4)
```
This scorecard shows SLA compliance by severity. Target: ‚â• 95% compliance for critical, ‚â• 90% for high.

Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
(a) **SLA breach detection**: identify a known vulnerability with `first_found` older than its SLA. Run the search. Confirm `sla_breach=1` and `days_overdue` is correct. Compare `age_days` with Tenable's vulnerability detail page.

(b) **Patch verification**: identify a vulnerability that was recently patched. Check Tenable ‚Äî the state should change to "fixed" after the next scan. Run the UC search before and after the scan. Confirm the vulnerability disappears from the results after the scan confirms the fix.

(c) **Exception register test**: add a known plugin_id/host combination to the exception register. Run the search. Confirm the vulnerability no longer appears. Set `exception_until` to yesterday. Run again. Confirm it reappears.

(d) **Severity-SLA mapping**: verify all severity levels have SLA entries: `| inputlookup nis2_patch_sla.csv`. All four levels (critical, high, medium, low) should have `sla_days` populated.

(e) **Tenable UI parity**: in Tenable.io, go to Vulnerabilities > Active > filter by "Critical" > sort by "Age". The oldest critical vulnerability should match the highest `age_days` in the Splunk results for `severity=critical`.

(f) **Asset enrichment coverage**: `index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-7d | lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS host OUTPUT category | stats count(eval(isnotnull(category))) as enriched count as total | eval enrichment_pct=round(100*enriched/total,1)`. Target: ‚â• 90% enrichment. Low enrichment means many vulnerability hosts aren't in the ES asset table.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Vulnerability and Patch Management`):
‚Ä¢ **Row 1 ‚Äî SLA compliance**: Single-value `Critical SLA compliance %` (green ‚â• 95%). Single-value `Overdue critical vulns` (red ‚â• 1). Single-value `Total active vulns`. Single-value `Average age (days)` for critical vulns.
‚Ä¢ **Row 2 ‚Äî Severity scorecard**: from the extended search, table with severity, total vulns, overdue, at-risk, compliance %. Colour gradient.
‚Ä¢ **Row 3 ‚Äî Overdue vulnerability detail**: table of all `sla_breach=1` vulnerabilities with `host`, `plugin_name`, `severity`, `age_days`, `sla_days`, `days_overdue`, `owner`, `bunit`. Sorted by `days_overdue` descending.
‚Ä¢ **Row 4 ‚Äî Remediation trend**: `index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-90d | eval week=strftime(_time,"%Y-W%V") | stats dc(eval(if(state="open",plugin_id,null()))) as opened dc(eval(if(state="fixed",plugin_id,null()))) as fixed by week | eval net=opened-fixed`. Line chart showing weekly open vs fixed ‚Äî the gap should be narrowing.
‚Ä¢ **Row 5 ‚Äî Top 10 oldest vulns**: table of the 10 oldest open vulnerabilities with `host`, `plugin_name`, `severity`, `age_days`, `cve`. These are the highest-risk items requiring immediate attention.

Runbook (owner: Vulnerability Management Team Lead):
1. Daily: review overdue vulnerabilities. For each critical SLA breach:
   - Contact the asset owner. Determine the remediation plan: is a patch available? Is testing underway? Is a compensating control in place?
   - If no patch exists (zero-day or vendor-dependency): create an exception register entry with a compensating control description, approved by the CISO, time-bound to the vendor's expected patch date.
   - If the patch is available but not deployed: escalate to the change management team. Request an emergency change if the vulnerability is actively exploited (CISA KEV catalog or EPSS score > 0.5).
2. Weekly: review the severity scorecard. Present to the CISO with trends.
3. Monthly: review exception register. Expired exceptions should be remediated or renewed with fresh risk acceptance.
4. Quarterly: present the patch management compliance report to the board as part of the NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) evidence package.

Step 5 ‚Äî Troubleshooting

‚Ä¢ **`first_found` timestamp format varies between Tenable.io and Tenable.sc.** Tenable.io uses ISO 8601 (`2026-04-15T10:30:00Z`), while Tenable.sc may use epoch seconds (`1744712400`). The `strptime` will fail for the wrong format. Fix: use `eval first_found_epoch=if(match(first_found,"^\d+$"), first_found, strptime(first_found, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"))` to handle both formats.

‚Ä¢ **Hostname normalisation mismatch.** Tenable reports `HOSTNAME` in uppercase (`SRV-WEB-01`), while the ES asset table uses lowercase (`srv-web-01`). Fix: normalise before lookup: `eval host=lower(coalesce(host, hostname, fqdn))`.

‚Ä¢ **Vulnerability count inflation from plugin updates.** When Tenable updates a plugin (e.g., adds a new CVE to an existing plugin), it may create a new detection event with the current timestamp as `first_found`, resetting the age counter. Fix: use `stats min(first_found) as first_found by host plugin_id` to get the true first detection time across all detection events.

‚Ä¢ **SLA breach count includes informational vulns.** If `severity=info` detections are included, they inflate the total and lower compliance percentages. Fix: filter early: `where severity IN ("critical","high","medium","low") AND severity!="info"`.

‚Ä¢ **ServiceNow change ticket correlation fails.** The vulnerability doesn't link directly to the patch change ticket. Fix: use temporal and host-based correlation: `| join host type=left [search index=itsm sourcetype=snow:change_request category="Patching" earliest=-14d | rename cmdb_ci AS host]`. This links vulnerabilities to patch change tickets for the same host within the SLA window.

‚Ä¢ **Scanner coverage gaps ‚Äî hosts not scanned.** Some hosts may not be scanned by Tenable (offline, firewalled, out of scope). These hosts have zero vulnerabilities in Splunk but may actually be vulnerable. Fix: complement this UC with an asset coverage check: `| inputlookup asset_lookup_by_str | join type=left key [search index=vulnerability sourcetype=tenable:vuln earliest=-30d | stats latest(_time) as last_scan by host | rename host as key] | where isnull(last_scan) OR (now()-last_scan) > 2592000`.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long serious security holes have stayed open on each computer. When something important has waited too long, we raise it so the people responsible for fixing it can act before auditors or criminals force the issue.
